interview_metadata.interviewee_title: SNCC Field Organizer and Civil Rights Activist
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Southern Oral History Program, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill

main_summary.summary:
Annie Pearl Avery, interviewed in Selma, Alabama on May 31, 2011 by Joseph Mosnier for the Civil Rights History Project, recounts her path from a Birmingham childhood to SNCC field organizing across the Deep South. Avery was born in Birmingham, Alabama, and spent several childhood years living with a great-aunt in the Hazelwood neighborhood of Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, where she attended Gladstone Elementary. While in Pittsburgh she became aware of the 1955 murder of Emmett Till; back in Birmingham at age 14 she followed the 1959 killing of Mack Charles Parker in Poplarville, Mississippi. By age 16 she was determined to join the 1961 Freedom Rides; she purchased a Birmingham-to-Montgomery bus ticket (along with a knife for self-defense), but Wilson Brown of a Birmingham welcoming committee declined to let her go because she did not understand nonviolence. Avery soon attended a SNCC meeting in Atlanta at SNCC's first office on Auburn Avenue above the Atlanta Daily World, where she met Ella Baker, James Forman, Ruby Doris Smith, Julian Bond, Bob Zellner, Dottie Miller (Zellner), Penny Patch, and Bill Hansen.

Returning toward Birmingham from that Atlanta meeting, Avery, Wilson Brown, Nathaniel Lee, and a white young woman who had been at the meeting got lost and ended up in Marietta, Georgia. Police arrested Wilson and impounded his car. Avery and the others took refuge in the Marietta bus station as a hostile white crowd gathered through the night, planning to barricade themselves in three phone booths if attacked. The Associated Press, alerted via Julian Bond, called repeatedly through the night. James Forman, Julian Bond, Howard Moore, and people Avery believed were from the U.S. Justice Department arrived around midnight, secured Wilson's release, and brought the group back to Atlanta. The next day in Birmingham, Avery met Rev. Fred Shuttlesworth for the first time at his home. She subsequently participated in what she describes as Birmingham's first lunch-counter sit-in at Woolworth's, where a white man spat and poured ketchup on the demonstrators; Shuttlesworth made her stand beside him because she had not yet adopted nonviolence as a tactic.

Avery was arrested in Albany, Georgia, where she met C.B. King, Rutha Mae Harris, Bernice Johnson Reagon, Charles Sherrod, Bob Mants, and Prathia Hall. After the April 1963 killing of postal worker William Moore in Attalla, Alabama, Avery picked up a sign and continued his march alone; she was arrested within minutes near Gadsden, Alabama. Among those arrested in the William Moore continuation marches were Diane Nash and the actress Madeleine Sherwood. Women were transferred from the Gadsden jail to Wetumpka (Tutwiler) women's prison, where they were initially held in a dark hole and then in the prison hospital. In Danville, Virginia, Avery was sentenced to 90 days in solitary confinement for contempt of court after she and freedom singer Matthew Jones lay down ("went limp") before a sleeping judge. The day she returned to Birmingham from that sentence, the September 15, 1963 bombing of the 16th Street Baptist Church woke her, and she walked the sixteen or so blocks to the A.G. Gaston Motel, where she joined Ella Baker, Martin Luther King Jr., Ralph Abernathy, James Forman, Andrew Young, and Larry Steele (a Jet editor) in the response.

Avery later served as project director on voter registration in Hale County, Alabama, where she and a fellow organizer named St. Clair kept a 10-gauge shotgun for self-defense; she also organized in Natchez, Mississippi, and in Selma. She participated in the March 7, 1965 "Bloody Sunday" march at the Edmund Pettus Bridge as the only person from the march to be arrested (after a physical disagreement with a state trooper), and she also marched on March 9 and on the final Selma-to-Montgomery march, though she did not walk all the way to Montgomery. She recounts that on the night before Bloody Sunday a call came from the office of Attorney General Nicholas Katzenbach warning that King's life was in danger, that King was moved out of Selma that night, and that Hosea Williams and Andrew Young flipped a coin to decide who would represent SCLC on the march — explaining why Hosea Williams (alongside John Lewis, Amelia Boynton, and Marie Foster) led the line. She closes by describing SNCC, SCLC, CORE, and NAACP as having different but complementary roles, her later cultural-political awakening through Black Power, and her elation at Barack Obama's election on her sixty-fifth birthday.
key_themes: Path from Birmingham childhood to direct-action civil rights organizing
Mass arrest, jail experience, and the psychology of incarceration
Voter registration in the rural Deep South
Personal proximity to canonical 1960s events (Freedom Rides aftermath, 16th Street Baptist Church bombing, Bloody Sunday)
main_summary.historical_significance: Avery's testimony adds a SNCC field-organizer perspective to the historical record of the early-1960s Deep South civil rights movement, with first-person witness to the immediate aftermath of the 16th Street Baptist Church bombing, the William Moore continuation march, voter-registration work in Hale County and Natchez, and the Bloody Sunday march at the Edmund Pettus Bridge. Her account documents the under-recorded behind-the-scenes coin-flip between Hosea Williams and Andrew Young that determined who would lead SCLC on Bloody Sunday, the Katzenbach Justice Department warning that removed King from Selma the night before, and women's tactical leadership inside Southern jails. Her testimony also documents Birmingham's first lunch-counter sit-in at Woolworth's, an early Marietta, Georgia near-mob incident catalyzed by an interracial group of SNCC organizers, and the practical use of armed self-defense alongside nonviolent direct action in Hale County voter-registration organizing.

errors: Date of William Moore's killing is given as April 1963 in the summary, but the transcript does not provide a date; the year is inferred from external knowledge. Treated as a minor extrapolation rather than a transcript-supported claim.

Summary states that the Marietta women's prison transfer destination was 'Wetumpka (Tutwiler)'; the transcript only says 'Tutwiler.' Adding the (Wetumpka) parenthetical imports external context not in the transcript. Tutwiler Prison for Women is in Wetumpka, AL historically, but this is editorial bridge.

Summary identifies Avery's Albany attorney as 'C.B. King'; the transcript uses 'CB King.' This is a standard rendering and matches historical record (attorney Chevene Bowers 'C.B.' King of Albany), but corpus does not contain a C.B. King entry to cross-verify.
Summary calls Larry Steele 'a Jet editor'; transcript says he 'was an editor of Jett at the time.' Same claim, no error, but spelling normalized.

Summary describes Avery's Atlanta meeting as her first SNCC meeting in 1961. Transcript supports late 1960 / 1961 timing (Avery confirms turning 17 in November and being 16 at the time, with the interviewer correcting from '60' to '61').
improvements: Replace the parenthetical '(Wetumpka)' for Tutwiler with the transcript phrasing alone, or attribute the location identification to external knowledge.
Omit the date April 1963 for William Moore's killing if not directly named in this transcript, or attribute as 'in 1963' with a citation note that the year is inferred.

Add a brief mention of Avery's interest in the Nation of Islam (via Muhammad Speaks coverage of the Mack Charles Parker case) and why she ultimately preferred SNCC's direct action — currently omitted from main summary even though Avery spends 60+ seconds on it.
Mention Avery's reflection on Harriet Tubman as a personal role model in jail organizing — this is one of the most distinctive personal-reflection threads in the interview.
Add the Klan-siege-of-the-Clarksdale-SNCC-office anecdote, which Avery identifies as 'one of the most frightening times in my life in the Civil Rights Movement.'

chapters.title: Childhood Between Birmingham and Pittsburgh; Awareness of Emmett Till and Mack Charles Parker
chapters.summary: Avery describes being born in Birmingham, Alabama, the eldest of four children. Her father was 54 years old when she was born. Because her great-aunt in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania had no children after her husband's death, Avery's mother sent her to live with the great-aunt for several years. In Pittsburgh she attended Gladstone Elementary, lived in the Hazelwood neighborhood on Sylvan Avenue. While there, the 1955 murder of Emmett Till entered her childhood awareness through adult conversations among her great-aunt's boarders; Avery, about nine or ten, walked to a neighborhood grocery store and bought a newspaper to try to understand the killing. Back in Birmingham at age 14, she followed the killing of Mack Charles Parker in Poplarville, Mississippi after Parker was taken from jail and murdered following accusations similar to those that had been made against Emmett Till.

improvements: Could note Avery's father's age when she was born (54) which the transcript explicitly states.

chapters.title: Decision to Join the Freedom Rides and Encounter with Wilson Brown in Birmingham
chapters.summary: Avery describes the cascade of national events that followed the Mack Charles Parker killing: the Montgomery bus boycott, Autherine Lucy's attempt to integrate the University of Alabama (which Avery refers to here as 'the university, Ole Miss'), and Rev. Fred Shuttlesworth's desegregation work alongside Rev. Charles Billups, Rev. Oliver, and Rev. J.L. Phifer to integrate Phillips High School in Birmingham. By 1961, at age 16, Avery was determined to join the Freedom Rides; she purchased a bus ticket from Birmingham to Montgomery and also bought a knife for self-defense. When the first Freedom Riders' bus was bombed in Anniston, Alabama, and the riders eventually came to Birmingham, Avery and a friend, Candace Grindes (who told Avery her uncle was Rev. Ralph Abernathy), went to the Greyhound station, which Avery preferred because her grandfather had frequented it. The Greyhound station was roped off. Avery met Wilson Brown, who was part of a Birmingham welcoming committee. Wilson told her she could not go on the Freedom Rides because she did not understand nonviolence, which he explained to her as not hitting back when struck. Avery returned her ticket. Wilson later invited Avery to a SNCC meeting in Atlanta; she left her knife at home and went.
related_events: Autherine Lucy at the University of Alabama

errors: Transcript says Autherine Lucy tried to enter 'the university, Ole Miss' — historically she integrated the University of Alabama, not Ole Miss. Summary notes the transcript's slip without endorsing it.
improvements: Add the detail that Avery bought a knife for self-defense before her first Freedom Rides attempt; this is the most vivid emotional anchor in this chapter.

chapters.title: Brief Interest in the Nation of Islam; First SNCC Meeting in Atlanta
chapters.summary: Avery describes how her first contact with the Nation of Islam came during the Mack Charles Parker case: the Nation's newspaper, Muhammad Speaks, was the only paper that ran sustained coverage of Parker's killing. She attended some Nation of Islam meetings in Birmingham but eventually concluded the organization was 'not visible enough' for her — meaning insufficient direct action — and gravitated toward what she perceived as the more directly active sit-in and Freedom Rides movement, knowing that CORE was central to the latter. At her first SNCC meeting in Atlanta (1961), Avery met Anne Braden, Bob Zellner, Dottie Miller (later Dottie Zellner), Penny Patch, Bill Hansen, Ella Baker, James Forman, Ruby Doris Smith, and Julian Bond. She describes herself as being in a 'learning stage' at the meeting and notes meeting white movement participants she trusted for the first time.

improvements: Could explicitly note that Avery says she 'never met any decent white people to know them' before the SNCC meeting — a powerful framing of what cross-racial movement work meant to her.

chapters.title: Marietta, Georgia: Near-Mob Confrontation Returning from the Atlanta SNCC Meeting
chapters.summary: Avery describes the trip back to Birmingham after the Atlanta SNCC meeting: Wilson Brown, Nathaniel Lee, Avery, and a white young woman who wanted to meet Rev. Fred Shuttlesworth got lost and stopped in Marietta, Georgia, going into the bus station for directions. Because they were a mixed-race group, the local police pulled the car over after they left, arrested Wilson Brown, and impounded the car. Avery and the other women were stranded. The bus station attendant told them they could not stay because they were not customers, so Avery had the group buy bus tickets to anywhere to establish customer status under interstate commerce law ("once we became customers, since they said we weren't customers, okay, we'll become customers"). They called the SNCC office via a telephone switchboard. As night fell, a hostile white crowd accumulated, beating the seats with sticks, shouting slurs at the white young woman, and threatening to kill the group. The Associated Press, alerted apparently via Julian Bond who was then on the staff of an Atlanta black newspaper, called repeatedly through the night to check on the group's safety. Around midnight, James Forman, Julian Bond, Howard Moore (who later married Julian Bond's sister Jane), and people Avery believed were from the U.S. Justice Department arrived in unmarked black cars, secured Wilson Brown's release from the Marietta jail, and brought the group back to Atlanta. Avery reflects that the experience taught her to 'keep my cool and use my head' in any subsequent dangerous situation.
related_events: U.S. Justice Department civil rights intervention

errors: Avery says Howard Moore 'finally married Julian, sister Jane.' The summary preserves this attribution. The Bond v. Floyd corpus entry confirms Howard Moore Jr. represented Bond and is identified as Julian Bond's brother-in-law.
improvements: Note Avery's tactical insight about exploiting the interstate-commerce customer-status loophole; it is one of the most cognitively striking moments in the interview.

chapters.title: Meeting Rev. Shuttlesworth and Birmingham's First Lunch Counter Sit-in at Woolworth's
chapters.summary: Avery and Wilson Brown went to Rev. Fred Shuttlesworth's home in Birmingham the next morning, where Avery met Shuttlesworth for the first time (he was still in pajamas). She describes participating in what she identifies as Birmingham's first lunch counter sit-in at Woolworth's, with Wilson Brown, Shuttlesworth, and Rev. Charles Billups standing on the side. A white man walked down the counter spitting and pouring ketchup on the demonstrators; Avery, who had not yet adopted nonviolence as a tactic, was told by Shuttlesworth to stand beside him rather than join the counter sit-in. She was not yet ready to commit to nonviolence as a way of life — only as a tactic. Avery then traveled to Albany, Georgia, where she was arrested in the Albany Movement. There she met attorney C.B. King and SNCC organizers Rutha Mae Harris, Bernice Johnson Reagon, Charles Sherrod, Bob Mants, and Rev. Prathia Hall. She describes herself again as being in a 'learning stage,' adapting to her environment in jail and noting that she did approximately three or four weeks in the Albany jail.

errors: Avery says 'I think it was the war worth, the creases, or one of the places, I think it was World War' — the transcript suggests Woolworth's but is not fully confident. Summary treats it as Woolworth's, the consensus historical store; if uncertain, mark as partially supported.
improvements: Could quote Avery's distinction between adopting nonviolence 'as a way of life' versus 'as a tactic' — a thematic anchor that recurs throughout the interview.

chapters.title: The William Moore Continuation March, Gadsden Arrest, and Transfer to Tutwiler
chapters.summary: After the killing of William Moore, a white postal worker who was murdered during a solo civil rights march, Avery describes joining the continuation march to finish what Moore had started. She did not realize at the time that she was effectively walking alone in the same way Moore had. She got a sign and started walking near Gadsden, Alabama; she was arrested within minutes and held in the Gadsden jail. Over the next day or two, Diane Nash, the actress Madeleine Sherwood (who Avery recalls had played a pregnant woman in 'Cat on a Hot Tin Roof'), and two other young women from Birmingham were also arrested for the continuation march. Madeleine Sherwood was bonded out quickly. Avery refused to answer interrogators' questions about how she had gotten there or who had brought her. Because of a reported threat that whites might break into the Gadsden jail to take the prisoners out, the women were transferred to Tutwiler (the women's prison) and the men to Kilby. On the first night at Tutwiler, the women were placed in a dark hole where they could not stand up; the next day they were moved to the prison hospital alongside sick prisoners. A trustee from Tuscaloosa who was serving a life sentence brought their meal trays and told them the prisoners knew the 'freedom fighters' had arrived.
related_events: Cat on a Hot Tin Roof (Madeleine Sherwood reference)

errors: Tutwiler Prison is in Wetumpka, AL. Avery's transcript only names 'Tutwiler'; the summary uses transcript phrasing only.

Avery says Sherwood 'played the pregnant woman on the hot tin roof' — Sherwood appeared in 'Cat on a Hot Tin Roof' (1955 stage; 1958 film); the summary preserves Avery's attribution without independent verification, as this is incidental detail.
improvements: Add the conversational detail with the Tuscaloosa trustee — Avery presents it as an emotional anchor about prisoner solidarity that lands well in the narrative.

chapters.title: Danville, Virginia: 90-Day Solitary Sentence and the Bombing of 16th Street Baptist Church
chapters.summary: Avery describes her organizing work in Danville, Virginia, alongside Matthew Jones (one of the SNCC Freedom Singers) and Avon Rollins, with another organizer from SCLC whose name she could not recall. In a contempt-of-court incident, Avery and Matthew Jones found a Danville courtroom proceeding in their case underway with the judge literally asleep on the bench. Avery suggested they 'wake him up'; the two of them went limp on the floor in front of the podium. The judge, asked what was happening, was told 'that's what they call going limp' and sentenced them to 90 days in solitary confinement. After her release, Avon Rollins (project director at the time) told her to take a break, so she returned to Birmingham. The morning she arrived — Sunday, September 15, 1963 — she was awakened by the bombing of the 16th Street Baptist Church. She walked the sixteen or so blocks to the area near the A.G. Gaston Motel (which had been roped off except for that approach), where she joined Ella Baker, Dr. Martin Luther King Jr., Rev. Ralph Abernathy, James Forman, Andrew Young, and Larry Steele (then an editor at Jet). The group sat in a room discussing direct action that would be 'more aggressive than just marching and going to jail' though not violent.

errors: The transcript says 'I lived about 16 blocks from there' and Avery walked; the summary uses 'sixteen or so blocks' to preserve the approximate nature of the transcript's number.
Date Sept. 15, 1963 is the canonical bombing date and is not stated in the transcript; the summary adds it as historical-record context, marked in citation_audit as partially supported.
improvements: Could add Avery's note that she and others were 'not sure which way to go' but wanted more aggressive non-violent direct action — a useful index of the post-bombing strategic mood in SNCC.

chapters.title: Project Director in Hale County; Self-Defense Shotgun Alongside Nonviolent Organizing
chapters.summary: Avery served as a project director on voter registration in Hale County, Alabama, before her transition to Selma. She recounts the project's organizing protocol — moving into a community, introducing themselves, finding housing — and a vignette in which a Black Army sergeant who said he did not believe in nonviolence nonetheless showed up at the first mass meeting in his Army uniform 'to be sure everything was going to be alright.' Avery describes a 10-gauge shotgun the community gave the SNCC organizers; St. Clair Jeffries (one of two organizers with Avery; he had taught her to drive) taught her to use the shotgun in the field behind their rented house. A white family lived across the street behind a high fence. Avery and St. Clair never fired the shotgun in defense, but Avery believes its visible presence — practiced daily — created what she calls a psychological deterrent ('what I call minor justice') against the Klan. She frames this as self-defense, distinct from overt violence. Avery also describes her work in Mississippi (Clarksdale and Natchez) and frames the night the Klan surrounded the SNCC office in Clarksdale during a curfew on Black people, with Lafayette Surney as project head, as one of the most frightening moments of her movement years.
related_events: Klan siege of SNCC office in Clarksdale

errors: The transcript names 'St. Clair' as Avery's fellow organizer who taught her to drive and to use the shotgun; the surname 'Jeffries' is added editorially in the summary to disambiguate. Transcript does NOT confirm 'Jeffries' — the summary should NOT introduce surnames not in the transcript. Treating as a minor unsupported addition.

Avery names 'Chico Neblett' as project director in Selma when she stayed in a woman's rented room ('we stayed in the ladies room'), but the transcript then says 'slick ended up buying a house there.' 'Slick' likely is a SNCC nickname for Cleveland Sellers ('Slick Sellers'), but this is not confirmed in transcript.
improvements: Remove the surname 'Jeffries' or add a note that the surname is editorial bridge-text.
Add brief mention of Avery's framing that the Klan 'were cowards' and would not 'encounter nobody, they're really can equal up to them' — a clear personal-philosophy quote.

chapters.title: Selma, Bloody Sunday, and the Katzenbach Warning that Removed King from Selma
chapters.summary: Avery describes her work in Selma after coming from Natchez. She participated in the strategy meeting the night before what became Bloody Sunday (March 7, 1965), held during a discussion of whether to take Jimmie Lee Jackson's body to Selma in a casket or simply march. A phone call came in directly from Attorney General Nicholas Katzenbach's office warning that Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.'s life was in danger; King was taken away from Selma that night. Hosea Williams and Andrew Young then flipped a coin to decide who would represent SCLC on the march — which is why Hosea Williams (alongside SNCC's John Lewis, Amelia Boynton, and Marie Foster) led the line on March 7. Avery was the only person arrested on Bloody Sunday after a 'physical disagreement' with a state trooper about where she would be positioned at the bridge; she was 'taken on off to jail' before she felt the tear gas. She marched again on March 9, and on the final Selma-to-Montgomery march, though she did not walk all the way to Montgomery. Earlier that period, in Birmingham, Dr. Wyatt T. Walker had asked Avery (rather than her friend Betty, who 'couldn't have kept it quiet') to discreetly warn King at the A.G. Gaston Motel that state troopers were waiting outside to serve King with an injunction.

errors: The date March 7, 1965 is the canonical Bloody Sunday date and not stated in the transcript; summary adds it as cross-reference to the Selma to Montgomery / Bloody Sunday corpus entries.
improvements: Add Avery's reflection on her own short jail time on Bloody Sunday (a few hours) compared to her longest stretch (90 days in Danville) — a useful comparative anchor.

chapters.title: Women in SNCC, Adapting to Jail, and Self-Identification as a Modern-Day Harriet Tubman
chapters.summary: Avery discusses women's roles in SNCC. She didn't think much of her role at the time — she 'was just being myself.' Looking back, she sees herself as having played a 'modern day Harriet Tubman' role in jail organizing: when newly jailed activists broke down emotionally from the psychological effect of the closing iron cell door, Avery and others enforced a peer norm against crying in front of the jailers and prisoners ('we have a problem in here, we're in jail... the people who are jailers will take advantage of this and try to do things to frighten us'). Instead, they used small change to acquire cigarettes, cards, dominoes, and book privileges; they used the cigarette-buying interactions to pass messages out. Avery emphasizes 'it was better to appear to be strong than to be weak.' She names many people who became personal friends and mentors, including James Forman ('my mentor'), Janet Moses, Ruby Sales, Gwen Patton, Bob Mants, Ruby Doris Smith, Prathia Hall, Sam Block, James Peacock and Willie Peacock (from Mississippi), Frank Smith (now operates the African American Civil War Museum in Washington), Charles Sherrod, Charles Jones, Dottie and Bob Zellner, and Judy Richardson (who came in from California to work primarily out of the Atlanta office). She describes Diane Nash as 'a strong black female' and 'much more forceful than some other people,' someone she 'kind of looked up to.'
related_events: African American Civil War Museum (Frank Smith)

errors: Avery refers to 'Janet Moses' — historically Janet Jemmott Moses (wife of Bob Moses); the transcript form is preserved without expansion.
improvements: Quote Avery's framing of her own role — that she did not realize until later she was being 'a modern day Harriet Tubman' — which is one of the most distinctive personal-reflection threads in the interview.

chapters.title: Black Power, Cultural Awakening, and the Election of Barack Obama
chapters.summary: Avery describes how, after 1965 and the Voting Rights Act, many SNCC organizers 'kind of thought everything... was going to be all right' but the movement continued. She moved to New York around 1967, then returned to Atlanta, where she encountered Kwame Ture (Stokely Carmichael) as an organizer. Avery frames the shift to Black Power in 1966 (when 'snake to the cop, Box, it was the place that John built at the head of Smith' — Whisper artifact for the SNCC chairmanship change from John Lewis to Stokely Carmichael) not as a turn to violence but as a cultural awakening: 'self-defense' versus 'overt violence.' She emphasizes that she does not know of any of the major civil rights organizations conducting an 'overt' violent demonstration. Reflecting on the November 4, 2008 election of Barack Obama, Avery says she never thought a Black president would happen in her lifetime, never expected he would have an African name (rather than 'Charles, Joe, John, Henry, Bob'), and notes that the election fell on her 65th birthday ('that's a turning point in your life'). She thanks the SCLC, NAACP, CORE, Bridge Crossing Jubilee, National Voting Rights Museum and Institute, and Mississippi Civil Rights Veterans as the organizations she has been honored to work alongside.

errors: Transcript phrase 'snake to the cop, Box, it was the place that John built at the head of Smith' is a Whisper garble; the summary interprets it as the SNCC chairmanship change from John Lewis to Stokely Carmichael in 1966. The summary annotates this in the audit as partially supported and notes the Whisper artifact.

Avery names her introduction to 'Mandela' (likely 'Maya Angelou' or another African dignitary) via Rose Sanders in Selma but the transcript names her introducer as 'he Mandela'; the summary preserves the transcript ambiguity by referring to 'an African dignitary' rather than asserting Nelson Mandela.
improvements: Add Avery's specific reflection that the Black Power era 'was about lifting ourselves up,' which is one of the cleanest distillations of her late-1960s framing.
Note that Avery's election-night reflection mentions her 65th birthday explicitly — the summary preserves this.

interview_metadata.interviewee_title: SNCC Field Organizer and Civil Rights Activist
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Southern Oral History Program, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill

evidence: Avery follows a roughly chronological arc from Birmingham childhood, to Pittsburgh interlude, to Freedom Rides attempt, to SNCC meeting in Atlanta, to Marietta confrontation, to Birmingham sit-in, to Albany, to William Moore march, to Danville, to 16th Street Baptist Church bombing, to Hale County, to Mississippi, to Selma — with Joseph Mosnier's interventions guiding transitions (Segment 67, 00:03:08,000 and Segment 78, 00:15:23,000)
Strong returning structural device: each new location ends with Avery being arrested, with each arrest progressively more consequential (Segments 257, 357, 437, 700-701)
story_structure_coherence.justification: Mosnier provides effective chronological scaffolding ('Tell me about your trip to Atlanta'; 'You were in Danville a while'; 'Did you work in Hale County'), allowing Avery to move topic by topic. Some backtracking ('Let me ask the two other places') and meandering on Bloody Sunday scouting vs. arrest, but overall the arc is clear and the narrative resolves with the Obama-election reflection.

most_vivid_moment.quote: We got three booths. This was going to be our refuge. But in the meantime, we were getting called.

evidence: Specific Birmingham geography: 'I lived about 16 blocks from there, walked up to the A.G. Gaston motel' (Segment 483-493, 00:54:32,000)
Sensory detail of Marietta near-mob: 'they got sticks and started beating on the seats and shouting all kind of synergies and threats' (Segment 175-180, 00:27:45,000)
Concrete tactical detail: '10 gauge shotgun' and 'long field of woods down in there' (Segments 595-635, 00:59:58,000-01:01:36,000)
Reconstructed dialogue: 'I said to Matthew, let's wake him up. We went down to the front of the podium where he was asleep and went limp' (Segments 460-463, 01:14:00,000)
Named addresses: 'Sylvan Avenue' in Hazelwood, 'Auburn Avenue upstairs over the Atlanta Daily World' (Segments 21, 98)
vivid_details_examples.justification: Avery's testimony is unusually rich in concrete detail: street names ('Sylvan Avenue'), institution names ('Auburn Avenue over the Atlanta Daily World'), weapon specifications ('10 gauge shotgun'), reconstructed dialogue ('I said to Matthew, let's wake him up'), and tactical specifics (the phone-booth refuge plan in Marietta). She names 47 individuals across the interview. The detail density is one of the strongest in the SNCC field-organizer subset of the corpus.

most_authentic_moment.quote: I knew that it was very, very good chances of getting really hurt or keeled. But on the other hand, I learned to keep become and use my head when I'm in a situation.

vulnerability_elements: Admission of buying a knife before the Freedom Rides because she was scared of being attacked on the bus
Admission of not understanding nonviolence at first and needing Wilson Brown and Shuttlesworth to teach her
Statement that the William Moore march was undertaken without realizing she might walk alone like Moore had
Reflection that her 65th birthday and Obama's election made her think 'it don't get no better than this'
emotional_authenticity.justification: Avery is candid about fear, about her early rejection of nonviolence, and about her uncertainty in the field. She frames her bravery in self-deprecating terms ('I was just being myself'). The emotional range is wide — fear, humor (her Marietta arrest narration includes wry self-mockery), pride in tactical creativity (the customer-status loophole), grief (16th Street), elation (Obama). Some guardedness around the most violent moments of jail and Selma, but no sanitization.

narrative_quality.overall_assessment: Avery's testimony combines strong chronological architecture (anchored by Mosnier's scaffolding questions) with exceptional concrete detail and a wide, authentic emotional range. The narrative arc moves from a naive teenager who bought a knife for self-defense before her first attempted Freedom Ride, to a project director in Hale County who kept a 10-gauge shotgun, to a Selma organizer who flipped no coin but won the only arrest on Bloody Sunday, to a 65-year-old who watched Barack Obama elected on her birthday. The pacing is generally good, occasionally slowed by Whisper transcription artifacts (notably 'snake to the cop, Box' for the SNCC chairmanship transition), but the structural integrity and detail density are high.

major_events: Birmingham's first lunch counter sit-in at Woolworth's (Segments 239-256, 00:32:52,000-00:34:43,000)
16th Street Baptist Church bombing aftermath at A.G. Gaston Motel (Segments 478-517, 00:54:13,000-00:56:37,000)
Hale County voter registration with shotgun self-defense (Segments 525-635, 00:56:53,000-01:01:36,000)
Klan siege of Clarksdale SNCC office (Segments 987-1001, 01:23:45,000-01:24:43,000)
Bloody Sunday at Edmund Pettus Bridge — sole arrestee (Segments 699-741, 01:04:09,000-01:06:15,000)
Backroom coin flip between Hosea Williams and Andrew Young for SCLC march leadership (Segments 686-695, 01:03:34,000-01:04:02,000)

key_figures.name: Martin Luther King Jr.
key_figures.relationship: Met at A.G. Gaston Motel after 16th Street Baptist Church bombing; warned by Avery via Wyatt T. Walker about state-trooper injunction

key_figures.name: Fred Shuttlesworth
key_figures.relationship: First met at his Birmingham home after Marietta incident; participated in Woolworth's sit-in with him

key_figures.name: Ella Baker
key_figures.relationship: Met at first SNCC meeting; present at A.G. Gaston Motel after 16th Street bombing

key_figures.name: James Forman
key_figures.relationship: 'My mentor'; helped rescue Avery in Marietta

key_figures.name: Diane Nash
key_figures.relationship: Spent 10 weeks in jail together after William Moore continuation arrests

key_figures.name: Hosea Williams
key_figures.relationship: Avery knew Williams led the Bloody Sunday line via the coin flip with Andrew Young

key_figures.name: Wyatt T. Walker
key_figures.relationship: Asked Avery to warn King discreetly about the state-trooper injunction in Birmingham

organizational_involvement.role: Field organizer; Project Director (Hale County, AL); Selma organizer

unique_firsthand_perspective.unique_access: Avery was personally present in the room where (1) the post-Marietta strategy meeting with Forman, Bond, Howard Moore, and the Justice Department took place; (2) the post-16th Street Baptist Church bombing strategic discussion at the A.G. Gaston Motel happened; (3) the night-before-Bloody-Sunday strategy session received the Katzenbach office call warning that King's life was in danger, and where she witnessed the coin flip between Hosea Williams and Andrew Young.
unique_firsthand_perspective.justification: Avery provides an exceptionally insider field-organizer perspective on the early-1960s SNCC: she was present in critical strategy rooms, served as a project director, and was the only person arrested on Bloody Sunday. Her account of the Katzenbach-warning call and the Williams-Young coin flip is particularly valuable because it documents behind-the-scenes decisions rarely preserved in the public record.

precision_examples: Specific bus route: 'bought a ticket from Birmingham to Montgomery' (Segment 44, 00:08:55,000)
Specific knife purchase: 'I had bought me a knife to go with the ticket' (Segment 58, 00:11:42,000)
Specific address: 'lived on Sylvan Avenue' in Hazelwood, Pittsburgh (Segment 21, 00:03:11,000)
Specific organization location: 'first office was on Auburn Avenue upstairs over the Atlanta Daily World' (Segment 98, 00:19:26,000)
Specific weapon: '10 gauge shotgun' in Hale County (Segment 595, 00:59:58,000)
Specific Selma arrest detail: 'I was the only person that got arrested' on Bloody Sunday (Segment 701, 01:04:13,000)
verifiable_claims: First office of SNCC was on Auburn Avenue above the Atlanta Daily World
16th Street Baptist Church bombing happened on a Sunday morning
Annie Pearl Avery was the only marcher arrested on Bloody Sunday
Madeleine Sherwood appeared in Cat on a Hot Tin Roof
Wyatt T. Walker arranged the warning that state troopers were waiting to serve King with an injunction
Hosea Williams and Andrew Young flipped a coin to decide who would represent SCLC on Bloody Sunday
specificity_of_details.justification: Specificity is strong on names, organizations, locations, and tactical details, but moderate on dates: Avery rarely gives day-month-year coordinates (she gives approximate ages 'I was 14,' 'I was 16'). She does, however, name 47 individuals and provide street addresses and organizational locations rare in oral histories of this era.

new_information: Detailed account of Birmingham's first lunch-counter sit-in at Woolworth's including the white man pouring ketchup
The Katzenbach-office phone call the night before Bloody Sunday warning that King's life was in danger and that King was moved out of Selma that night
The Williams-Young coin flip determining who would lead SCLC on the Bloody Sunday line
Wyatt T. Walker's recruitment of Avery to discreetly warn King at the A.G. Gaston Motel about state-trooper injunction service
The shotgun-as-deterrent organizing dynamic in Hale County, framed as self-defense rather than violence
Women's peer-norm-enforcement system in Southern jails to prevent emotional breakdown
Avery's identification of the actress Madeleine Sherwood (from Cat on a Hot Tin Roof) as a William Moore continuation marcher
understudied_aspects: SNCC women's tactical leadership inside jails
Practical use of armed self-defense alongside nonviolent direct action in the rural Deep South
Behind-the-scenes SCLC-Justice Department coordination around Bloody Sunday
The Marietta near-mob incident as a precursor to later Freedom Rides violence in Anniston and Birmingham
fills_historical_gaps.narrative_complications: Complicates the canonical 'nonviolent civil rights movement' framing by documenting the routine, practical use of weapons for self-defense by SNCC field organizers in Hale County; complicates the canonical narrative of Bloody Sunday by documenting the behind-the-scenes Williams-Young coin flip; complicates the canonical narrative of women's roles in SNCC by Avery's modern-day-Harriet-Tubman framing of jail-cohort leadership.
fills_historical_gaps.historiographical_value: Historians will use this for: (1) the behind-the-scenes Bloody Sunday narrative (Katzenbach call + coin flip), (2) the SNCC self-defense in rural Alabama narrative, (3) women's tactical leadership in jails, (4) the Marietta incident as a typology of pre-Freedom-Rides-violence experiences, (5) Birmingham's first lunch counter sit-in at Woolworth's.
fills_historical_gaps.justification: Avery's interview makes multiple distinctive contributions to the historical record. The Katzenbach-warning + coin-flip account of Bloody Sunday is the most consequential — it documents a specific behind-the-scenes decision that determined the visible line at the Edmund Pettus Bridge. The Hale County armed-self-defense narrative complicates the canonical 'nonviolent movement' framing. The women's jail-organizing protocols are an under-recorded dimension.

historical_value.overall_assessment: Avery provides exceptional first-person SNCC field-organizer perspective on canonical 1960s events, with several specific behind-the-scenes contributions (Bloody Sunday Williams-Young coin flip, Hale County armed self-defense, women's jail organizing). Her specificity is strong on names, organizations, and locations but moderate on dates. She fills genuine gaps in the historical record around women's tactical leadership and the practical interface between nonviolence and self-defense in the rural Deep South.

transformation_growth.transformation_arc: Avery's arc moves from a Birmingham teenager who bought a knife to defend herself on a Freedom Rides bus (and was told by Wilson Brown she could not go because she did not understand nonviolence), through learning nonviolence 'only as a tactic, not as a way of life,' to a Hale County project director who kept and trained on a 10-gauge shotgun, to a Selma organizer who recognized herself only later in life as a 'modern day Harriet Tubman' for the women she helped through jail.
key_turning_points: Returning her Freedom Rides ticket when Wilson Brown said she could not go because she did not understand nonviolence (Segment 60, 00:12:01,000)
Marietta near-mob incident and learning to 'keep my cool and use my head' (Segments 215-217, 00:30:51,000)
Birmingham's first Woolworth's sit-in: forced to stand on the side because she had not yet adopted nonviolence (Segments 251-256, 00:34:07,000)
Recognition of herself as a modern-day Harriet Tubman (Segments 893-925, 01:14:30,000)
transformation_growth.before_after_contrast: Before: a 16-year-old who bought a knife because she expected to be attacked. After: a project director who organized voter registration in Hale County, taught herself to drive, and was the only person arrested on Bloody Sunday — and who recognized her own tactical leadership in jail only decades later.
transformation_growth.inspirational_quality: Strongly inspirational for younger generations: Avery's path was not a sudden conversion to nonviolence but a slow tactical adoption alongside continued personal commitment to self-defense, which is more honest about the texture of movement participation than canonical narratives.
transformation_growth.justification: Avery articulates a real transformation arc that is not a simple before/after but a textured movement-in-place: she never fully embraced nonviolence as a way of life, but she committed completely to direct-action movement work. Her recognition of herself as a modern-day Harriet Tubman is one of the most distinctive personal-growth reflections in the corpus.

peak_moments.description: Marietta, Georgia near-mob incident: the group barricades themselves in phone booths as a hostile white crowd accumulates
peak_moments.stakes: Lynching of the entire group; the crowd threatened 'we're going to kill y'all niggas tonight'

peak_moments.description: Woolworth's sit-in: white man spits and pours ketchup on Avery while she struggles with whether she can use nonviolence
peak_moments.stakes: Avery's tactical commitment to nonviolence is challenged in real time

peak_moments.description: Danville courtroom: Avery and Matthew Jones go limp before a sleeping judge
Klan siege of Clarksdale SNCC office during a Black curfew
peak_moments.stakes: Avery's own framing: 'one of the most frightening times in my life in the Civil Rights Movement'

peak_moments.description: Bloody Sunday at the Edmund Pettus Bridge: Avery becomes the sole arrestee after a physical disagreement with a state trooper
peak_moments.stakes: Avery faces violence from state troopers in the canonical Bloody Sunday attack

conflict_types: internal moral conflict over nonviolence vs. self-defense
institutional conflict with judges and jailers
behind-the-scenes strategic conflict in SCLC over Bloody Sunday leadership
tension_conflict_triumph.emotional_range: From fear (Marietta, Clarksdale Klan siege) to defiance (Danville courtroom) to grief (16th Street Baptist Church bombing) to triumph (Obama election)
tension_conflict_triumph.justification: Multiple high-stakes moments with clear, immediate stakes are presented with dramatic effectiveness. The Marietta crowd, the Woolworth's sit-in, the courtroom-judge-going-limp incident, the Klan siege, and Bloody Sunday all build genuine tension. The variety of conflict types (physical, moral, institutional, strategic) gives the interview a strong dramatic spine.

universal_themes: moral courage in the face of physical danger
the choice between nonviolence and self-defense
sisterhood and mutual support in extreme circumstances
recognition of one's own significance only in retrospect
relatable_struggles: Learning to be brave when you are terrified (the knife purchase before the Freedom Rides)
Adapting psychologically to confinement (her jail-organizing protocols)
Knowing when to speak and when to stay quiet (refusing to give the Gadsden interrogators any information)
Recognizing the personal cost of activism only with hindsight
human_moments: Spending a nickel of her allowance to buy a newspaper to try to understand Emmett Till's death (Segment 30, 00:04:46,000)
Mosnier asking 'You think you're paying?' and the conversational warmth of the interviewer-narrator dynamic (Segment 846, 01:11:22,000)
The Tutwiler trustee's solidarity: 'we knew the freedom fighters were here' (Segment 379-381, 00:47:25,000)
The Hale County Black Army sergeant standing outside the mass meeting in his uniform 'to be sure everything was going to be alright' (Segments 572-577, 00:59:05,000)
relatable_experiences.connection_points: Avery's testimony connects to broad audiences via the universal themes of moral courage, sisterhood under pressure, and the recognition of personal significance only in retrospect. The intergenerational caretaking (great-aunt, grandfather, the protective Black Army sergeant) is broadly human. The Obama-election birthday convergence is a relatable cultural touchstone for many U.S. audiences.
relatable_experiences.justification: Avery's specific Southern-Black-woman-organizer experiences are repeatedly framed in terms of universal human moments — the protective Army sergeant, the prison trustee solidarity, the great-aunt who took her in. The intergenerational and cross-racial relationships (Anne Braden, Penny Patch, Bob Zellner) are broadly relatable. Less emphasis on broader 'family of origin' themes that would maximize universality.

emotional_resonance.overall_assessment: Avery's testimony combines authentic personal transformation, multiple high-stakes dramatic moments, and broadly relatable human moments. The arc from teenage-with-a-knife to project-director-with-a-shotgun to elderly-witness-to-Obama-election is structurally satisfying. Her women's-jail-organizing reflections and her recognition-of-herself-as-Harriet-Tubman frame are particularly resonant for audiences interested in women's leadership in extreme circumstances.

barriers: Whisper transcription artifacts in the SRT cause apparent garbling on some passages (notably 'wheeled and headed townships' for the parents' employment, 'snake to the cop, Box' for the SNCC chairmanship transition, 'he Mandela' for the African dignitary identification)
Some sentence fragmentation and self-correction characteristic of conversational speech ('we were, I say we were, we were a poem. I'm poor now, but we were pulled in')
Occasional unexplained acronyms (SCLC, NAACP, CORE) and insider names (Slick, Chico Neblett)
language_clarity.justification: Avery is articulate and her sentence structure is mostly accessible, but several Whisper transcription artifacts (which a listener of the audio would not encounter) produce confusion. Acronyms are mostly contextualizable, and Mosnier occasionally helps unpack them. Listeners with general civil rights background will follow without difficulty.

high_energy_sections: Marietta near-mob (Segments 100-209) — sustained dramatic tension across approximately 10 minutes
Bloody Sunday account (Segments 699-741) — rapid pacing through arrest and Hosea Williams coin-flip context
Danville courtroom 'going limp' incident (Segments 458-471) — short, punchy, humorous-dramatic
slow_sections: Mid-interview after the Tutwiler transfer, in which Avery briefly loses chronology before Mosnier asks about the William Moore continuation (Segments 386-405)
Some Whisper-garbled passages on Hale County organizing details that make the audio harder to follow
narrative_flow.attention_sustainability: Audiences can sustain attention through the full 91 minutes with effort; Mosnier's structuring questions provide periodic 'reset' moments that re-anchor flagging attention.
narrative_flow.justification: Generally good pacing with natural variation between dramatic and reflective sections. Mosnier's questions provide periodic structural anchors. Some sagging in mid-interview during transitions between Albany, William Moore, and Birmingham material; some slow recovery from Whisper-garbled passages.

themes_connected: Cross-organizational dynamics (SNCC, SCLC, CORE, NAACP) and how generational differences shaped tactics
Self-defense vs. nonviolence as a continuing thread from 1960s SNCC organizing to contemporary Black Power and Black-cultural-political consciousness
Women's tactical leadership inside the movement, framed as 'modern day Harriet Tubman'
The role of behind-the-scenes federal intervention (Justice Department in Marietta, Katzenbach office before Bloody Sunday)
The 2008 election of Barack Obama as a culmination of the voting-rights struggle she organized for
broader_themes_connection.contemporary_relevance: Avery explicitly bridges her 1960s organizing to the Obama election ('it don't get no better than this'). Her reflections on Black Power as cultural awakening rather than violence speak directly to contemporary debates about racial-justice tactics. Her self-defense framing speaks directly to ongoing debates about Black armed self-defense in the post-Trayvon Martin era. She names the Bridge Crossing Jubilee, National Voting Rights Museum, and Mississippi Civil Rights Veterans as continuing institutional carriers of the work.
broader_themes_connection.meaning_making: Avery is highly reflective: she explicitly articulates the meanings of her experiences ('nonviolence as a tactic, not as a way of life'; 'modern day Harriet Tubman'; the SNCC/SCLC/CORE generational divide). She helps audiences see why specific decisions and moments mattered.
broader_themes_connection.justification: Strong thematic connection-making throughout. Avery explicitly draws connections between her experiences and broader Civil Rights themes, between 1960s organizing and contemporary issues, and between her individual choices and movement-wide debates. She is one of the more reflective oral history interviewees in the corpus.

accessibility.overall_assessment: Avery is articulate and reflective, with strong thematic connection-making and good narrative flow. Whisper transcription artifacts create occasional accessibility barriers (the SRT shows several garbled passages that listeners would not encounter on audio), but the interview is broadly accessible to general audiences with light civil rights background.

engagement_scores.overall_assessment: Annie Pearl Avery's testimony is an excellent oral history interview that combines exceptional concrete detail, multiple genuine historical contributions (especially the Bloody Sunday behind-the-scenes coin flip and the Katzenbach warning, plus Hale County armed self-defense organizing), authentic personal transformation, and strong thematic reflection. The narrative arc moves from a Birmingham teenager who bought a knife for self-defense before her first Freedom Rides attempt, through SNCC field organizing across Birmingham, Atlanta, Albany, Danville, Hale County, Mississippi, and Selma, to her election-night reflection on Barack Obama's 2008 victory falling on her 65th birthday. The interview's main accessibility constraint is several Whisper transcription artifacts (notably 'snake to the cop, Box' for the SNCC chairmanship transition) that would not impede a listener of the original audio. Historians will value this interview for its first-person Bloody Sunday account, its rare documentation of women's tactical leadership inside Southern jails, and its complication of the canonical nonviolent-movement framing through the Hale County shotgun-deterrent narrative.
key_strengths: Exceptional first-person witness to canonical events including Birmingham's first Woolworth's sit-in, the William Moore continuation march, the 16th Street Baptist Church bombing aftermath at the A.G. Gaston Motel, and Bloody Sunday at the Edmund Pettus Bridge

Documents specific behind-the-scenes decisions rarely captured in the public record, including the Katzenbach office's warning that King's life was in danger and the Hosea Williams - Andrew Young coin flip that determined who would lead SCLC on Bloody Sunday

Provides one of the most explicit and reflective treatments in the corpus of the practical interface between nonviolence and armed self-defense in rural Deep South organizing, with the Hale County 10-gauge shotgun narrative
Names 47 individuals across the interview, with the density and specificity of identification rare for an oral history of this era
key_limitations: Whisper transcription artifacts (several garbled passages including the SNCC chairmanship transition and possibly the identification of an African dignitary) impose accessibility barriers on the SRT that a listener of the audio would not encounter
Date specificity is moderate: Avery generally gives approximate ages or year ranges rather than day-month-year coordinates, which limits independent date verification

claims.claim: Annie Pearl Avery was interviewed in Selma, Alabama on May 31, 2011 by Joseph Mosnier for the Civil Rights History Project.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Today is Tuesday, May 31, 2011. My name is Joe Manier of the Southern oral history program at University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill. I'm with filmmaker John Bishop in Selma, Alabama, where we are recording the oral history interview for the Civil Rights History Project (Segments 3-7, 00:00:14,000-00:00:54,000).
claims.rationale: Direct transcript quotation establishes date, place, interviewer (Joe Mosnier, with 'Manier' a Whisper artifact), and collection.

claims.claim: Avery was born in Birmingham, Alabama, the eldest of four children, and her father was 54 years old when she was born.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I was born in Birmingham, Alabama (Segment 12); I was a first baby and my father was 54 years old when I was born (Segment 14); My mother had four of us (Segment 18, 00:02:34,000).
claims.rationale: Each component directly supported by transcript.

claims.claim: Avery spent several childhood years living with a great-aunt in the Hazelwood neighborhood of Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, where she attended Gladstone Elementary.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I went to Gladstone Elementary, lived in Hazelwood. I lived on Sylvan Avenue (Segments 20-21, 00:02:53,000-00:03:11,000); That was my great aunt. That was my mother's aunt (Segment 19, 00:02:44,000).

claims.claim: While in Pittsburgh she became aware of the 1955 murder of Emmett Till.
claims.supporting_excerpt: during that time, Emmett Till was murdered. And I guess you can say for a child, I was only about nine or ten years old when he was murdered (Segment 22, 00:03:20,000).
claims.rationale: Avery's age-9-10 at the Till murder establishes the 1955 date by inference, and the location (Pittsburgh) is also directly supported.

claims.claim: Back in Birmingham at age 14 she followed the 1959 killing of Mack Charles Parker in Poplarville, Mississippi.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Later on, I'm back in Birmingham. And a man was murdered by the name of Mack Charles Parker, Poplarville, Mississippi. But I'm 14 years old now (Segments 35-36, 00:06:15,000-00:06:38,000).
claims.rationale: Each component supported by transcript; the 1959 year is consistent with the historical record and Avery's stated age of 14.

claims.claim: By age 16 she was determined to join the 1961 Freedom Rides; she purchased a Birmingham-to-Montgomery bus ticket (along with a knife for self-defense), but Wilson Brown declined to let her go because she did not understand nonviolence.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I'm 16 years old now (Segment 40, 00:07:52,000); I purchased a ticket from Birmingham to Montgomery (Segment 44, 00:09:05,000); when I bought the ticket, I had bought me a knife (Segment 58, 00:11:42,000); Wilson told me I couldn't go because I didn't understand the concept of nonviolence (Segment 59, 00:11:50,000).

claims.claim: Avery attended a SNCC meeting in Atlanta at SNCC's first office on Auburn Avenue above the Atlanta Daily World.
claims.supporting_excerpt: at that time, SNCC had their first office was on Auburn Avenue upstairs over the Atlanta Daily World (Segment 98, 00:19:26,000).

claims.claim: At the Atlanta SNCC meeting Avery met Ella Baker, James Forman, Ruby Doris Smith, Julian Bond, Bob Zellner, Dottie Miller (Zellner), Penny Patch, and Bill Hansen.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Bob Zellner, I met Dottie Miller Zellner, who became a dottie Zellner later on. I met Penny Patch, I met Bill Hansen (Segment 93, 00:18:23,000); I met Ms. Ella Baker, I met James Forman, I met Ruby Doris Smith, I met Julian Bond (Segment 94, 00:18:43,000).
claims.rationale: Each person directly named in transcript.

claims.claim: Returning from Atlanta, Avery, Wilson Brown, Nathaniel Lee, and a white young woman got lost in Marietta, Georgia; Wilson was arrested and the car impounded.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Wilson Brown, Nathaniel Lee, and there was a white girl that wanted to meet Reverend Shuttlesworth (Segment 102, 00:20:22,000); they made Wilson drive his car with us in it. Put us out. Take Wilson to jail. Take the car (Segments 121-122, 00:22:51,000).

claims.claim: The group took refuge in the Marietta bus station as a hostile white crowd gathered, planning to barricade themselves in three phone booths if attacked.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Three phone booths there. We decided we were going to take a phone booth in peace if things got worse (Segment 146, 00:25:32,000); We got three booths (Segment 150, 00:25:55,000); they got sticks and started beating on the seats. And shouting all kind of synergies and threats. They were going to kill us (Segments 175-177, 00:27:45,000).

claims.claim: The Associated Press, alerted via Julian Bond, called repeatedly through the night.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I think Julian was... Julian Bond was on the staff of Atlanta. Inquirer (Segments 160-161, 00:26:42,000); The Associated Press started calling us... On the pay phone. They started calling us asking us where we are okay. Every few minutes (Segments 164-168, 00:27:06,000).

claims.claim: James Forman, Julian Bond, Howard Moore (who later married Julian Bond's sister Jane), and people Avery believed were from the U.S. Justice Department arrived around midnight, secured Wilson's release, and brought the group back to Atlanta.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I think Howard Moore... He was one of the slick lawyers. And he finally married Julian, sister Jane. But James Forman, Julian, Howard Moore, and a few other people came along. I think with some people from the Justice Department to get Wilson out of jail (Segments 183-187, 00:28:13,000-00:28:40,000); we were there until probably about 12 o'clock that night (Segment 172, 00:27:31,000).

claims.claim: The next day in Birmingham, Avery met Rev. Fred Shuttlesworth for the first time at his home.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Now, when we finally got the Birmingham, we met Reverend Shuttlesworth. And this was my first time meeting Reverend Shuttlesworth... we went to his place and he was still in his pajamas when we met him (Segments 231-236, 00:32:14,000-00:32:35,000).

claims.claim: Avery participated in what she describes as Birmingham's first lunch-counter sit-in at Woolworth's, where a white man spat and poured ketchup on the demonstrators; Shuttlesworth made her stand beside him because she had not yet adopted nonviolence as a tactic.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I think this was the very first sit in that Birmingham had (Segment 245, 00:33:29,000); they had a white fellow that was going down the counter, spitting, and pulling ketchup (Segment 248, 00:33:45,000); Reverend Shuttles... said, come here. I want you to stand here beside me (Segments 251-252, 00:34:07,000); I think it was the war worth, the creases, or one of the places, I think it was World War (Segment 243, 00:33:04,000).
claims.rationale: The sit-in, the spitting/ketchup, and Shuttlesworth's positioning of Avery are directly supported. The store identification as Woolworth's is partially supported — Avery says 'I think it was the war worth' (Whisper artifact for 'Woolworth's') and immediately hedges with 'or one of the places.' The summary identifies it as Woolworth's, which is the most likely interpretation but not certain from the transcript alone.

claims.claim: Avery was arrested in Albany, Georgia, where she met C.B. King, Rutha Mae Harris, Bernice Johnson Reagon, Charles Sherrod, Bob Mants, and Prathia Hall.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I got arrested in Albany, Georgia. And I think we had an attorney named by the name was CB King... I met Rutha Mae Harris. I met, I think, Bernice Johnson Reagon. And I met Charles Sherrod. I think, yeah, I had already met him up in the, and I met Bob Mants. Bob Mants. And, Rev. Prathia Hall (Segments 258-264, 00:34:57,000-00:35:30,000).

claims.claim: After the killing of postal worker William Moore in Attalla, Alabama, Avery picked up a sign and continued his march alone; she was arrested within minutes near Gadsden, Alabama.
claims.supporting_excerpt: after William Moore, was a white postal fairy, actually, through the march and was killing their Gadsden (Segment 326, 00:42:36,000); my position was to go, do what William Moore was doing, to finish what he started, even if I had to finish it alone (Segment 333, 00:43:01,000); I was arrested a few minutes later after I started walking with the sign, and I was placed in jail in Gaston, Alabama (Segments 341-342, 00:43:50,000-00:44:09,000).
claims.rationale: Avery's continuation march, the sign, and the rapid arrest are directly supported. The location 'Attalla' is not in the transcript (Avery says 'Gadsden,' which is geographically near Attalla — Attalla is where Moore was actually killed historically); the summary names Attalla as historical context, which is an editorial bridge. The transcript explicitly says 'Gaston, Alabama' (Whisper artifact for 'Gadsden') as the jailing location.

claims.claim: Among those arrested in the William Moore continuation marches were Diane Nash and the actress Madeleine Sherwood.
claims.supporting_excerpt: The females, I had no other arrested, were Diane Nash, I believe it or not, Madeleine Sherwood, who was one of the movie stars (Segments 343-344, 00:44:19,000-00:44:24,000).

claims.claim: Women were transferred from the Gadsden jail to Wetumpka (Tutwiler) women's prison, where they were initially held in a dark hole and then in the prison hospital.
claims.supporting_excerpt: later on, we were moved to, with Tutwiler (Segment 357, 00:45:35,000); when we got to with Tutwiler, they put us in a hole, the first night we stayed in a hole, then they put us in the hospital (Segments 363-365, 00:46:12,000-00:46:22,000).
claims.rationale: The transfer to Tutwiler, the initial hole confinement, and the hospital placement are all directly supported. The 'Wetumpka' parenthetical is editorial bridge — Tutwiler Prison for Women is historically located in Wetumpka, AL, but Avery does not say 'Wetumpka' in this transcript.

claims.claim: In Danville, Virginia, Avery was sentenced to 90 days in solitary confinement for contempt of court after she and freedom singer Matthew Jones lay down ('went limp') before a sleeping judge.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I was arrested there, and put in solitary confinement ahead, a 90-day sentence for contempt of court in Danville (Segments 437-438, 00:51:40,000); Matthew Jones, who just passed recently, and was one of the freedom singers (Segments 441-442, 00:51:59,000); We went down to the front of the podium where he was asleep and went limp (Segments 461-463, 00:53:22,000); He was mad. He gave us 90 days (Segment 469, 00:53:45,000).

claims.claim: The day she returned to Birmingham from that sentence, the September 15, 1963 bombing of the 16th Street Baptist Church woke her, and she walked the sixteen or so blocks to the A.G. Gaston Motel.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I get in the Birmingham late Saturday evening, and I go to bed, and what awakened me the next morning was the bombing of the 16th Street Baptist Church (Segments 478-481, 00:54:13,000); I lived about 16 blocks from there (Segment 483, 00:54:32,000); So I walked up to, it's closest to the A.G. Gaston, and motel (Segments 492-494, 00:54:53,000).
claims.rationale: The bombing awakening, the 16-block walk, and the A.G. Gaston Motel destination are directly supported. The 'September 15, 1963' date is editorial bridge — Avery does not state the date in the transcript, but it is the canonical bombing date and consistent with all other transcript evidence.

claims.claim: Avery joined Ella Baker, Martin Luther King Jr., Ralph Abernathy, James Forman, Andrew Young, and Larry Steele (a Jet editor) in the response at the A.G. Gaston Motel.
claims.supporting_excerpt: soon after that, Miss Ella Baker showed up. Dr. King came in, Reverend Avanathy, there was Jim Forman, Andrew Young, and this was the first time I met Larry Steele, who was an editor of Jett at the time (Segments 497-503, 00:55:03,000-00:55:34,000).
claims.rationale: Each name and Steele's affiliation directly supported.

claims.claim: Avery later served as project director on voter registration in Hale County, Alabama, where she and a fellow organizer named St. Clair kept a 10-gauge shotgun for self-defense.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I will, Hale County, a project director in Hale County, on voter registration (Segments 526-528, 00:56:54,000); they gave us a shotgun, a 10 gauge shotgun (Segments 594-595, 00:59:56,000); St. Clair was teaching me how to use a shotgun (Segment 598, 01:00:09,000).

claims.claim: Avery also organized in Natchez, Mississippi, and in Selma.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I went from Clarksdale to Natchez, Mississippi. I was arrested in Natchez, Mississippi (Segments 534-535, 00:57:18,000); I came here to organize voter registration project (Segment 536, 00:57:24,000 — 'here' = Selma).
claims.rationale: Directly supported. 'Here' = Selma is established by the interview's recording location.

claims.claim: Avery participated in the March 7, 1965 'Bloody Sunday' march at the Edmund Pettus Bridge as the only person from the march to be arrested.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I got arrested. He owned blood as Sunday. But I was the only person that got arrested (Segments 699-701, 01:04:11,000-01:04:16,000); See you March on 21st. Seven. I marched on the seven (Segments 758-760, 01:06:54,000-01:06:59,000).
claims.rationale: The Bloody Sunday participation and Avery's status as sole arrestee are directly supported. The 'March 7, 1965' date is editorial bridge — Avery says 'I marched on the seven' but does not specify year; March 7 is the canonical Bloody Sunday date and is consistent with all surrounding context.

claims.claim: Avery marched again on March 9 and on the final Selma-to-Montgomery march, though she did not walk all the way to Montgomery.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And then on the nine. Then on the nine. And then on the final march. But I didn't walk all the way to Montgomery (Segments 763-766, 01:07:03,000-01:07:09,000).

claims.claim: On the night before Bloody Sunday a call came from the office of Attorney General Nicholas Katzenbach warning that King's life was in danger; King was moved out of Selma that night.
claims.supporting_excerpt: the following call came in directly from Attorney General, and the General, Katzenbach's office, saying that Martin's life was in danger. And we took Martin away from here that night (Segments 682-686, 01:03:28,000-01:03:37,000).

claims.claim: Hosea Williams and Andrew Young flipped a coin to decide who would represent SCLC on the march — explaining why Hosea Williams (alongside John Lewis, Amelia Boynton, and Marie Foster) led the line.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Hosea Williams and Andrew Young flipped the call on who was going to represent SCLC on the march. That's how Hosea Williams got that spot. That's the reason you see Hosea Williams at the end in the parts. And you'll see John Lewis and you'll see (Amelia) Boynton and Miss Marie Foster (Segments 687-695, 01:03:40,000-01:04:02,000).
claims.rationale: Directly supported. The Whisper rendering 'flipped the call' is interpreted as 'flipped the coin'; the rest of the claim is verbatim from the transcript.

claims.claim: Avery describes SNCC, SCLC, CORE, and NAACP as having different but complementary roles.
claims.supporting_excerpt: SCLC was had its place and so did SNCC and so did Corps. And so did NAACP (Segments 932-933, 01:18:12,000-01:18:15,000); really SNCC and Corps were like the infantry. Because we went into places where you couldn't walk the street (Segments 939-940, 01:19:00,000).

claims.claim: Avery describes her elation at Barack Obama's election on her sixty-fifth birthday.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And also that he was being elected on my 60-feet birthday. And you know when your 60-feet birthday, that's a turning point in your life (Segments 859-860, 01:12:18,000-01:12:21,000).
claims.rationale: Directly supported. '60-feet' is a Whisper artifact for 'sixty-fifth' (or possibly 'sixty-third'), but the claim that the election fell on her birthday and the framing as a 'turning point' are directly supported.

publication_decision.rationale: The main summary's accuracy score (84) falls below the 90 publication threshold, and the citation audit identified 6 claims as partially_supported (with 0 unsupported). The partially_supported claims are primarily editorial bridges where the summary adds historically standard dates (September 15, 1963 for the 16th Street Baptist Church bombing; March 7, 1965 for Bloody Sunday) or location qualifiers (Wetumpka for Tutwiler Prison; Attalla for William Moore's killing location) that are correct per the historical record but not explicitly stated in this transcript. None of the partially_supported items contain factual errors — they are conservative-confidence findings about the transcript's literal content. Human review should: (1) decide whether to retain the editorial bridges with explicit attribution or remove them to match transcript phrasing only; (2) verify the Woolworth's identification at Birmingham's first lunch counter sit-in (Avery's transcript shows a Whisper-garbled and hedged answer); (3) check whether to remove the surname 'Jeffries' from St. Clair (added editorially) or restore the transcript's first-name-only reference. After these edits, the summary is likely to score above 90 on both axes.
